coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of hydroquinone using novel silicon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yingnan
Wang, Qinzhi
Guo, Shuwen
Jia, Pei
Shui, Yuhang
Yao, Siyu
Huang, Chen
Zhang, Min
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Hydroquinone (HQ) is a major phenol compound pollutant derived from industrial wastewater. Its content in environmental water is closely related to the water quality. Thus, it is in great need to develop facile, sensitive and selective detection methods for HQ. In this paper, an effective and selective HQ detection probe was fabricated based on novel silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), which were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Such SiQDs exhibited good water solubility and intensely green fluorescence. Interestingly, their fluorescence can be significantly quenched upon addition of HQ, which is attributed to the electron transfer from SiQDs to the generated 1,4-benzoquinone, and the inner filter effect of SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex. This SiQD probe can detect HQ in a wide concentration range from 6 to 100 μM, with a detection limit of 2.63 μM. More importantly, compared with other reported methods, this probe can eliminate the interference from catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS), and gives new insight on the discrimination HQ from its isomers. Furthermore, this fluorescent probe was also successfully applied for the determination of HQ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries, indicating the reliability and potential application of this fluorescent probe for monitoring HQ.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Hydroquinone
Isomers
The inner filter effect
Fluorescent probe
Water quality monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HQ in water samples
Construction of SiQDs-based fluorescent probe for the detection of HQ
Possible mechanism of the fluorescent probe
Analysis of HQ in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge thefinancial supports of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601 ) and Young Thousand Talented Program ( A279021724 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the detection of HQ.

Characterization of SiQDs. (a) TEM micrograph of SiQDs. (b) Particle size distribution histogram of SiQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption (black line), FL excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of SiQDs. The inset shows the photos of SiQD solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm). (d) FT-IR transmitted spectra of SiQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Survey XPS spectra of SiQDs (a). High-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (d), Si2p (e) spectra of SiQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of SiQDs in the presence of HQ, CC and RS.
(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of SiQDs at different excitations. (b) Time-dependent fluorescence responses of SiQDs at different vortex time.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of SiQDs in the presence of various concentrations of HQ. (b) Plot of fluorescence quenching value (F0-F) at 512 nm versus the concentrations of HQ. The inset is the linear fitting curve of (F0-F) with respect to the concentrations of HQ (6–100 μM).

The selectivity of the SiQDs-based probe for the detection of HQ over other interfering substances. DSA-K+ refers to 2,5-Dihydroxybenzenesulfonic acid-potassium salt. HQ-Cl refers to 2-chlorohydroquinone. The concentrations of all interferences are 90 μM.
Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence sensing system in presence of different concentrations of HQ.
Comparison of the analytical performance of several commonly selected methods for the determination of hydorquinone.

row.entry: Grapheme-chitosan composite film/ glassy carbon electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of hydroquinone in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of hydroquinone using novel silicon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydroquinone (HQ) is a major phenol compound pollutant derived from industrial wastewater. Its content in environmental water is closely related to the water quality. Thus, it is in great need to develop facile, sensitive and selective detection methods for HQ. In this paper, an effective and selective HQ detection probe was fabricated based on novel silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), which were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Such SiQDs exhibited good water solubility and intensely green fluorescence. Interestingly, their fluorescence can be significantly quenched upon addition of HQ, which is attributed to the electron transfer from SiQDs to the generated 1,4-benzoquinone, and the inner filter effect of SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex. This SiQD probe can detect HQ in a wide concentration range from 6 to 100 μM, with a detection limit of 2.63 μM. More importantly, compared with other reported methods, this probe can eliminate the interference from catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS), and gives new insight on the discrimination HQ from its isomers. Furthermore, this fluorescent probe was also successfully applied for the determination of HQ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries, indicating the reliability and potential application of this fluorescent probe for monitoring HQ.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroquinone (HQ), as a kind of phenolic compound, has been extensively applied in many industrial processes including coal-tar production, photographic developers and paper manufacturing [1]. However, HQ contamination has also been a more and more serious global problem nowadays, which could cause extensive damage to human’s health through dermal, oral and respiratory. It has been included in the lists of priority pollutants to be monitored in the aquatic environment by both of US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and European Union (EU) [2]. Thus, it is quite imperative to detect HQ in environmental water through a sensitive and selective method.

To date, many methods have been applied for the detection of HQ, which include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3,4], capillary electrophoresis [5] electrochemical methods [6–8], flow-injection chemiluminescence [9], solid-phase extraction [10], and so on. However, these methods usually required sophisticated instrumentation and tedious samples preparation procedures, which highly limited their practical applications. In comparison with these methods, fluorescence assays display unique advantages (easy monitoring, rapid responses and high simplicity), providing a better choice for the detection of HQ. For example, Ni et al. reported a carbon dot-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive determination of HQ [11]. Li and co-workers fabricated a novel fluorescent probe based on a grapheme quantum dot-enzyme hybrid system for the analysis of HQ [12]. However, catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) usually have huge interference on the detection results of HQ as their physical and chemical properties are similar to HQ. In addition, enzyme-based probes have been challenged for their high cost, easily-lost activity and weak stability, which would limit the application of these probes. Thus, it is extremely needed to develop a low-cost and highly selective fluorescent probe for the analysis of HQ in the presence of its two isomers.

Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), a novel type of photoluminescence (PL) nanoparticles, have attracted great research interest in past years, owing to their broad absorption spectrum, stable photoluminescence and good biocompatibility. They have been widely applied in fluorescence detection and fluorescence imaging. For example, Wang et al. synthesized imidazole-functionalized SiQDs for highly selective and sensitive detection of l-histidine [13]. Erogbogbo et al. attached biocompatible SiQDs to biomolecules and successfully utilized them to label cancer cells [14]. Nevertheless, most of the reported SiQDs were water-insoluble [15], which severely limited their further application.

Herein, novel SiQDs with good water-solubility were prepared through one-pot hydrothermal method using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) and HQ as the silicon source and reductant, respectively. The as-prepared SiQDs emitted bright green fluorescence and showed a sensitive and selective response to HQ. As shown in Fig. 1 , upon addition of HQ, the fluorescence of SiQDs was quenched, which was possibly due to electron transfer from SiQDs to the generated 1,4-benzoquinone, and the inner filter effect of the SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex. Even in the presence of HQ’s two isomers (CC and RS), there was no interference on the fluorescence results, which is the first report up to now. These signal change can be used for the qualitative recognition and quantitative analysis of HQ. Based on this, we developed a facile fluorescent method for the detection of HQ with high selectivity and sensitivity using the prepared SiQDs.

DAMO, CC, MnCl2, Na3PO4, and 2-chlorohydroquinone were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, FeCl3, Co(NO3)2, NaNO3, Na2SO4, Ni(NO3)2, Na2SO3, cupric acetate and sodium acetate were obtained from Guanghua technology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Zn(NO3)2, Na2S, NaClO and phenol were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). HQ, KI, Na2SeO3 and benzoic acid were supplied by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). RS was purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Al2(SO4)3 was purchased from Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 2,5-Dihydroxybenzenesulfonic acid-potassium salt was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Twice-distilled water was used through all experiments.

Transmission electron micrographs were obtained by the Hitachi-600 (Japan) transmission electron microscope. The size of SiQDs was measured using the Nano Measurer software. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was taken on a Vetex 70 FT-IR spectroscopy (Bruker Corp., Germsny). UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan) and a PE LS-55 spectrometer, respectively. Luminescence lifetime measurements were recorded with an Edinburgh FLS 980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.

SiQDs were synthesized according to the reported method with a minor modification [16]. In brief, 6 mL of DAMO and 30 mL of ultrapure water were mixed by stirring. Then, 33 mg of HQ was dissolved into the above solution and the mixture was continuously stirred for 5 min. Thereafter, it was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 4 h. The resultant dark brown mixture was purified through a dialysis bag (100–500 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h. Finally, the as-prepared SiQDs were stored at 4 ℃ before further study.

Fluorescence studies were performed under the following procedures. In brief, 1 mL of the diluted SiQD solution (50-fold) was mixed with 1 mL of various concentrations of HQ, and then the mixed solution was diluted to 3 mL with deionized water. Before subjected to fluorescence spectral measurements (λex = 410 nm), the mixed solution was shaken for 6 min to make sure that they reacted completely. For CC, RS or other interfering ions, HQ solution (1 mL) was replaced by the corresponded solution and the fluorescence measurement procedures were performed as described above. All experiments were replaced at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HQ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of this probe for the detection of HQ in real water samples (a local tap water sample and the West Lake water sample in Northwest A&F University) was tested with the standard addition method. First, these samples were filtered by filter papers to remove large particles. Then a series of different amounts of HQ were dissolved in the real water samples, and the subsequent fluorescence detection measurements were carried out as described above (Section 2.4).

As shown in TEM image (Fig. 2 a), uniform mono-dispersed SiQDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method. The particle size is mainly distributed in the range of 5–9 nm (Fig. 2b) and the mean particle diameter is 6.87 nm. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were employed to characterize the optical properties of SiQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c, two typical absorption bands centered at 290 and 410 nm are presented in the UV–vis spectrum of SiQDs (black line). The former is attributed to the π→π* transitions of CO bonds of SiQDs, and the latter may be originated from the trapping surface state [16,17]. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 410 nm is overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum (red line), which endows a strong fluorescence emission peak at 512 nm (blue line). The inset of Fig. 2c shows the photos of SiQD solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively. It can be observed that the solution of SiQDs is yellow in daylight and exhibits intensely green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, reflecting its potential application in fluorescence detection.

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded to characterize the surface groups of SiQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the characteristic peaks at 3435 cm−1 and 3250 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the NH in the -NH2 and OH groups [18], while the peak at 1638 cm−1 is attributed to the NH bending [19]. The peaks at 2932 and 1489 cm−1 are attributed to C–H unsaturated stretching vibration and C–H bending vibration, respectively [15,16]. The peak at 1585 cm−1 is characteristic of the NH bend in the amine molecules [20] and the strong signal at 1349 cm−1 is assigned to C–N stretching vibrations. The characteristic peaks at 1030, 1125 and 932 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of Si-O and C–N and Si-O in the Si-O-H group, respectively [21]. And the peak located at 693 cm−1 belongs to the secondary amine. The relatively broad peak at 781 cm−1 indicates the presence of NH wagging vibration [16]. Above results indicate that there is an abundance of −OH and -NH2 on the surface of SiQDs, which endow them with good water solubility and facilitate their applications as luminescent probes in aqueous media.

To further analyze the surface composition of SiQDs, XPS measurements were performed. Fig. 3 a shows the presence of C, N, O, and Si with atomic percentages of 59.11%, 12.45%, 20.45% and 7.98%, and the corresponding peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s and Si2p are located at 284.4, 398.5, and 531.2 and 101.4 eV, respectively. The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum of SiQDs (Fig. 3b) indicates the presence of C-Si (283.9 eV) [16], CC/C = C (284.6 eV), C–N (285.4 eV) and CO (287.4 eV) groups [22], respectively. The N1s spectrum of SiQDs (Fig. 3c) is composed of three peaks centered at 398.3 eV (N-Si), 399.0 eV (C–N-C) and 399.6 eV (C–N), suggesting that N exists in three forms [23]. Two peaks at 530.7 and 531.5 eV correspond to Si-O in the XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d), and the peak at 532.4 eV is assigned to C–O. The binding energy of Si 2p (Fig. 3e) is observed at 101.0, 101.8 and 102.5 eV, which are in accordance with SiC, SiN and SiO groups, respectively [24]. The XPS results confirm that SiQDs with good water solubility are successfully synthesized, which are in accordance with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of SiQDs-based fluorescent probe for the detection of HQ
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of SiQDs with good water-solubility motivated us to explore their promising application in fluorescent detection. Interestingly, we found that the SiQDs were sensitive to HQ and their strong fluorescence was obviously quenched upon addition of HQ, while two isomers of HQ (CC and RS) did not have influence on the fluorescence of SiQDs (Fig. 4 ). Thus, further efforts were made to construct the SiQD-based fluorescent probe for rapid discrimination and detection of HQ.

Firstly, the excitation wavelength of the SiQDs was optimized to obtain better analytical performance. As shown in Fig. 5 a, under different excitation wavelengths in the range of 350–460 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelength of SiQDs is nearly excitation-independent. The maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength are at 410 nm and 512 nm, respectively. Hence, 410 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the following experiments.

Reaction time is another significant factor influencing the accuracy and sensitivity of fluorescent probes. Before the fluorescence measurement, the systems containing SiQDs and HQ were shaken to accelerate the reaction. As can be seen in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensities of the systems containing SiQDs and HQ are time-dependent and reach stable values after a period of time. After shaken for 6 min, the fluorescence intensities of the systems with different concentrations of HQ maintain constants, indicating that the reactions have reached a balance. Therefore, 6 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for the following experiments.

Under the above optimized conditions, the analytical performance of this probe was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs at 512 nm decreases with increasing the concentration of HQ. There is a good linear relationship (Fig. 6b) between the relative fluorescence intensity (F0-F) and the concentration of HQ in the range of 6–100 μM. The linear regression equation is y = 1.274x+48.669, with a correlation of 0.995. The detection limit of 2.63 μM (0.29 mg/L) is obtained based on a 3δ/S (δ refers to the standard deviation of the blank signal and S represents the slop of the linear calibration plot), which is lower than the U.S. Environment Protection Agency estimated wastewater discharge limit of 0.5 mg/L [25]. In addition, compared to other analytical methods in Table 1 , the detection limit and linear range of this method are comparable to them, suggesting that our detecting system exhibits good analytical capability.

Before being applied in real sample detection, selectivity is also an important parameter to evaluate the performance of a new fluorescent probe. Fig. 7 shows effects of some metal ions and phenolic compounds on the analytical results, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, SeO3 2−, S2−, I-, Ac-, SO4 2−, NO3 −, ClO−, SO3 2−, PO4 3-, phenol, benzoic acid, CC, RS, 2,5-Dihydroxybenzenesulfonic acid-potassium salt and 2-chlorohydroquinone. It can be seen that 24 interfering substances have negligible or a slight influence on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs, and only HQ and its ortho-substituted (electron acceptor group) derivatives give a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs. These results indicate that our HQ sensing system possesses excellent selectivity and can detect HQ and its derivatives, which all exist in industrial wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, we investigate the possible reaction mechanism of this fluorescent probe. As we all know, HQ can be easily oxidized to 1,4-benzoquinone [11]. Upon addition of HQ to the SiQD solution, the color of SiQD solution obviously turned from faint yellow to red (the inset of Fig. S1). Such obvious change indicated that HQ was oxidized to 1,4-benzoquinone, and then 1,4-benzoquinone might formed quinone-amine or n-π clathrate with amino groups on the surface of SiQDs [31,32]. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Figure S1), a new absorption peak at 480 nm appeared after HQ was added into the SiQD solution, which provided a support for the above speculation [33]. The morphology of SiQDs treated with HQ was then characterized. As shown in Figure S2a and b, there are new nanoparticles with size of 50 nm and most of them aggregate. Some SiQDs are distributed inside or around the new nanoparticles (Fig. S2c, d). Such morphology is in sharp contrast with that of SiQDs. We presumed that the morphology change process proceeds as follows: (1) In the presence of SiQDs with abundant amino groups, HQ can be easily oxidized to 1,4-benzoquinone (Figure S3a); (2) the 1,4-benzoquinone can form quinone-amine with amino groups on the surface of SiQDs based on the Michael addition reaction (Fig. S3b) [34]; (3) the 1,4-benzoquinone can form n-π clathrate with amino groups on the surface of SiQDs via hydrogen bonds, and hydrogen bonds adopt two forms (Fig. S3c) [35]. One type is that two carbonyl groups of one 1,4-benzoquinone form two hydrogen bonds with two amino groups on one SiQD (Fig. S3c, Type Ⅰ). The other is that two carbonyl groups of one 1,4-benzoquinone form hydrogen bonds with two SiQDs, which will link the SiQDs together (Fig. S3c, Type Ⅱ). Based on the above interactions, SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex (SiQDs-quinone-amine or n-π clathrate) with large size and aggregated morphology formed. Owing to that 1,4-benzoquinone was a good electron acceptor, electrons transferred from amino groups of the photoexcited SiQDs to 1,4-benzoquinone, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of SiQDs [11].

In order to further explore the quenching mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements were performed. The fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. S4) are about 3.57 ns for free SiQDs, and 3.58 ns in the presence of HQ. In addition, after the addition of HQ to the SiQD solution, an obvious change of the absorption spectrum of the solution was observed (Fig. S1). Thus, the fluorescence quenching is reasonably ascribed to the static quenching based on the almost unchanged fluorescence lifetime and the changed UV–vis absorption. Moreover, the quenching effect can be expressed well by Stern-Volmer’s equation:
F 0
/F = 1 +
K SV
c q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
c q
where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of SiQDs in the presence and absence of HQ, respectively. Ksv is the quenching constant, and cq is the concentration of HQ. Kq is the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 8 , (F0-F)/F shows a linear relationship with the concentration of HQ. Ksv is about 0.0099 μM−1 based on the slope of the regression line. Taking the fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs (τ0 = 3.57 ns) and Ksv into account, Kq is calculated to be 2.77 × 1012 M−1s−1, which is higher than the maximum value for dynamic quenching effect (1.0 × 1010 M−1s−1) [36]. Both the stable fluorescence lifetime and high quenching rate constant indicate that the fluorescence quenching might be ascribed to static quenching effect [37].

Besides, it is noteworthy that there is almost a full overlap between the absorption spectrum of the SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex and the emission spectrum of SiQDs (Figure S5), indicating that the inner filter effect may be another mechanism for SiQDs fluorescence quenching [38,39]. Hence, this fluorescent sensing was based on the electron transfer from SiQDs to the generated 1,4-benzoquinone, and the inner filter effect of the SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HQ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the applicability of this proposed fluorescent probe in practical samples, it was applied for the determination of HQ in two water samples. The tap water was collected from the lab faucet and the lake water was obtained from the lake in our school. Then a series of different amounts of HQ were dissolved in the real water samples for the fluorescence detection measurements. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of HQ are ranged from 92.50% to 105.10%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are lower than 3.46%. It demonstrates that this fluorescent probe has relatively high reproducibility and precision for the determination of HQ.

In summary, novel SiQDs with good water-solubility was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method, and a facile fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of HQ was successfully developed based on the electron transfer from SiQDs to the generated 1,4-benzoquinone and the inner filter effect of the SiQDs-1,4-benzoquinone complex. Under the optimal condition, this SiQD-based probe can detect HQ as low as 2.63 μM, independent of interference of other metal ions and HQ’s isomers. In addition, this fluorescent probe was successfully applied for the detection of HQ in tap water and lake water samples and holds great potential in monitoring water quality.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge thefinancial supports of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601) and Young Thousand Talented Program (A279021724).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yingnan Liu received her master’s degree in Northwest A&F University for food science and engineering in 2017, and subsequently began to study for doctoral program at Northwest A&F University under the guidance of Prof. Li Wang. Her research is focused on the design and application of fluorescent probes.

Qinzhi Wang received his master’s degree in Ningxia University for food processing and safety in 2017. He is currently pursuing his doctor’s degree at Northwest A&F University. His research is focused on the spectrum detection of food safety factors.

Shuwen Guo received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2018. Then he joined the faculty at Northwest A&F University, where he is currently a lecturer. His research interests focus on improving food functional components based on host-guest interactions and supramolecular self-assembly.

Pei Jia received her Bachelor's degree in Jilin University in 2017. She is currently pursuing her master’s degree at Northwest A&F University under the supervision of Professor Li Wang. Her research interests focus on the synthesis of fluorescent probes.

Yuhang Shui received her Bachelor's degree in 2017 from Shaanxi Normal University. She is now pursuing her master’s degree in Northwest A&F University. Her current research interests focus on developing fluorescent probes.

Siyu Yao is now pursuing her Bachelor’s degree in the College of food science and engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, China. Her research interest focuses on the synthesis of fluorescent probes.
Chen Huang is currently pursuing her Bachelor’s degree in Northwest A&F University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Min Zhang received her Ph.D. degree from Harbin Institute of Technology in 2017. Then she joined the faculty at Northwest A&F University, where she is currently a lecturer. Her research interests focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials.

Li Wang received her Ph.D. degree from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2012. Then she started her postdoctoral study in Department of Chemical Engineering of Worcester Polytechnic Institute, USA. She joined Northwest A&F University in 2016 and became a professor in college of Food Science and Engineering. Her research interests cover the design and application of fluorescent probes based nanomaterials.
